message:
feat: project infrastructure + bigint/bytes types + Zig C ABI

- Add vitest benchmarks (create, read, write, array, materialize)
- Add Zig-side library (zerobuf.zig) with matching binary layout
- Add 18 C ABI exports for cross-language FFI (Go/Rust/Python/C)
- Add bigint (i64) and Uint8Array (bytes) type support
- Add Date → f64 epoch ms conversion
- Add Astro Starlight docs site (5 pages)
- Add CI workflow (build → test → bench) + docs deploy workflow
- Add LICENSE (MIT)
- 47 TS tests, 13 Zig tests, all passing

files.patch:
@@ -0,0 +1,58 @@
+name: CI
+
+on:
+  push:
+    branches: [main]
+  pull_request:
+    branches: [main]
+
+jobs:
+  test:
+    runs-on: ubuntu-latest
+    steps:
+      - uses: actions/checkout@v4
+
+      - uses: pnpm/action-setup@v4
+
+      - uses: actions/setup-node@v4
+        with:
+          node-version: 22
+          cache: pnpm
+
+      - uses: mlugg/setup-zig@v2
+        with:
+          version: 0.15.2
+
+      - run: pnpm install --frozen-lockfile
+      - run: pnpm build
+      - run: pnpm check
+      - run: pnpm test
+
+      - name: Zig tests
+        run: cd zig && zig build test
+
+  bench:
+    runs-on: ubuntu-latest
+    needs: test
+    steps:
+      - uses: actions/checkout@v4
+
+      - uses: pnpm/action-setup@v4
+
+      - uses: actions/setup-node@v4
+        with:
+          node-version: 22
+          cache: pnpm
+
+      - run: pnpm install --frozen-lockfile
+
+      - name: Run benchmarks
+        run: pnpm bench | tee bench-output.txt
+
+      - name: Post benchmark results
+        if: always()
+        run: |
+          echo '## Benchmark Results' >> $GITHUB_STEP_SUMMARY
+          echo '```' >> $GITHUB_STEP_SUMMARY
+          cat bench-output.txt >> $GITHUB_STEP_SUMMARY
+          echo '```' >> $GITHUB_STEP_SUMMARY

@@ -0,0 +1,43 @@
+name: Deploy Docs
+
+on:
+  push:
+    branches: [main]
+    paths: [docs/**]
+
+permissions:
+  contents: read
+  pages: write
+  id-token: write
+
+concurrency:
+  group: pages
+  cancel-in-progress: true
+
+jobs:
+  deploy:
+    runs-on: ubuntu-latest
+    environment:
+      name: github-pages
+      url: ${{ steps.deployment.outputs.page_url }}
+    steps:
+      - uses: actions/checkout@v4
+
+      - uses: actions/setup-node@v4
+        with:
+          node-version: 22
+
+      - name: Install and build docs
+        working-directory: docs
+        run: |
+          npm ci
+          npm run build
+
+      - uses: actions/configure-pages@v5
+
+      - uses: actions/upload-pages-artifact@v3
+        with:
+          path: docs/dist
+
+      - id: deployment
+        uses: actions/deploy-pages@v4

@@ -0,0 +1,76 @@
+---
+title: Getting Started
+description: Install zerobuf and create your first zero-copy object in WASM memory.
+---
+
+## Install
+
+```bash
+pnpm add zerobuf
+```
+
+## Basic Usage
+
+```typescript
+import { zerobuf } from "zerobuf";
+
+// Load your WASM module
+const wasm = await WebAssembly.instantiate(wasmBytes);
+const memory = wasm.instance.exports.memory as WebAssembly.Memory;
+
+// Create a zerobuf instance over shared memory
+const buf = zerobuf(memory, wasm.instance.exports.__heap_base.value);
+
+// Create objects directly in WASM linear memory
+const user = buf.create({
+  name: "alice",
+  scores: [95, 87, 92],
+  address: { city: "NYC", zip: "10001" },
+});
+
+// Read — lazy, decodes from WASM memory on access
+console.log(user.name);         // "alice"
+console.log(user.scores[0]);    // 95
+
+// Write — directly to WASM memory
+user.name = "bob";
+user.scores.push(88);
+
+// Add new properties
+user.email = "bob@example.com";
+```
+
+## Materialization
+
+If you read the same field many times (hot loops), materialize into a plain JS object first:
+
+```typescript
+const snap = user.materialize();
+
+// snap is a plain JS object — no Proxy overhead
+for (let i = 0; i < 10_000; i++) {
+  process(snap.name, snap.scores);
+}
+```
+
+## Wrapping WASM Output
+
+When your WASM function writes a result into memory, wrap the pointer as a Proxy:
+
+```typescript
+const resultPtr = wasm.instance.exports.computeResult() as number;
+const result = buf.wrapObject(resultPtr);
+
+// Lazy — only reads the fields you touch
+console.log(result.score);
+```
+
+## Memory Limits
+
+zerobuf auto-grows WASM memory using a doubling strategy. Cap it with `maxPages`:
+
+```typescript
+const buf = zerobuf(memory, 0, { maxPages: 2048 }); // max 128MB
+```
+
+Maximum is 65535 pages (4GB minus 64KB — avoids a Chrome overflow bug).

@@ -0,0 +1,65 @@
+---
+title: With Durable Objects
+description: Using zerobuf inside Cloudflare Durable Objects that run WASM.
+---
+
+## Overview
+
+Each Durable Object has its own WASM instance with its own `WebAssembly.Memory`. zerobuf wraps that memory, giving you structured zero-copy access to WASM data.
+
+## Pattern
+
+```typescript
+import { DurableObject } from "cloudflare:workers";
+import { zerobuf, type ZeroBuf } from "zerobuf";
+
+export class FragmentDO extends DurableObject<Env> {
+  private buf!: ZeroBuf;
+  private wasmExports!: WasmExports;
+
+  private async ensureInitialized() {
+    if (this.buf) return;
+    const instance = await WebAssembly.instantiate(wasmModule);
+    this.wasmExports = instance.exports as WasmExports;
+    this.buf = zerobuf(
+      this.wasmExports.memory,
+      this.wasmExports.__heap_base.value,
+    );
+  }
+
+  // Public method = RPC endpoint
+  async scan(request: ScanRequest): Promise<ScanResult> {
+    await this.ensureInitialized();
+
+    // WASM computes, writes result at a known pointer
+    this.wasmExports.executeScan(request.ptr);
+    const resultPtr = this.wasmExports.getResultPtr();
+
+    // Wrap as Proxy — lazy, zero copy
+    const result = this.buf.wrapObject(resultPtr);
+
+    // Materialize before returning over RPC
+    // (RPC serializes, so we need a plain JS object)
+    return result.materialize() as ScanResult;
+  }
+}
+```
+
+## Key Points
+
+**One zerobuf per DO instance.** Each DO has its own WASM memory. Create the zerobuf instance once in `ensureInitialized()`.
+
+**Materialize before RPC.** zerobuf Proxy objects reference WASM memory via absolute pointers. When you return data over DO RPC, call `.materialize()` to snapshot into a plain JS object that can be serialized.
+
+**Arena lifecycle.** WASM memory persists as long as the DO is alive (until hibernation evicts it). Use `arena.save()`/`arena.restore()` (planned) for per-request cleanup without resetting the entire heap.
+
+**No race conditions.** JS and WASM on the same DO never run concurrently — each RPC call runs to completion before the next one starts.
+
+## When to Use zerobuf vs Raw Buffers
+
+| Data type | Use |
+|---|---|
+| Query results, metadata, configs | zerobuf — structured, lazy access |
+| Typed columns (Float64Array, Int64) | Raw typed buffers — tighter packing, SIMD-friendly |
+| Strings, nested objects | zerobuf — handles encoding, growth |
+| Hot compute paths | Raw buffers for input, zerobuf for output |

@@ -0,0 +1,96 @@
+---
+title: With Zig
+description: Use zerobuf.zig to read/write shared objects from WASM Zig code.
+---
+
+## Overview
+
+The Zig-side library (`zerobuf.zig`) reads and writes the same binary layout as the JS-side. Both sides share one `WebAssembly.Memory` — no serialization, no copy.
+
+## Setup
+
+Copy `zerobuf.zig` into your Zig WASM project, or add it as a dependency in `build.zig.zon`.
+
+### Option A: Copy the file
+
+```bash
+cp path/to/zerobuf/zig/zerobuf.zig your-wasm-project/src/
+```
+
+### Option B: Zig package dependency
+
+```zig
+// build.zig.zon
+.dependencies = .{
+    .zerobuf = .{
+        .url = "https://github.com/teamchong/zerobuf/archive/main.tar.gz",
+    },
+},
+```
+
+## Reading Objects from JS
+
+JS creates an object with `buf.create({ x: 1.0, y: 2.0 })` and passes the handle pointer to WASM:
+
+```zig
+const zb = @import("zerobuf");
+
+// Exported function — JS calls this with the handle pointer
+export fn transform(handle_ptr: u32) void {
+    // Get a slice of WASM linear memory
+    const mem = getMemorySlice();
+
+    const obj = zb.ObjectReader.init(mem, handle_ptr);
+    const x = obj.getF64("x") orelse return;
+    const y = obj.getF64("y") orelse return;
+
+    // Write result back — JS sees it immediately
+    var writer = zb.ObjectWriter.init(mem, handle_ptr);
+    _ = writer.setF64("x", x * 2.0);
+    _ = writer.setF64("y", y * 2.0);
+}
+```
+
+## Reading Arrays
+
+```zig
+const arr = zb.ArrayReader.init(mem, handle_ptr);
+const len = arr.len();
+
+for (0..len) |i| {
+    const val = arr.get(@intCast(i));
+    switch (val) {
+        .f64 => |v| processFloat(v),
+        .i32 => |v| processInt(v),
+        .string => |v| processString(v),
+        else => {},
+    }
+}
+```
+
+## Writing Tagged Values
+
+```zig
+// Write directly into a 16-byte value slot
+zb.writeF64Val(mem, offset, 3.14);
+zb.writeI32Val(mem, offset, 42);
+zb.writeBool(mem, offset, true);
+zb.writeNull(mem, offset);
+
+// Reference types (string/array/object) need their header pointer
+zb.writeStringRef(mem, offset, string_header_ptr);
+zb.writeArrayRef(mem, offset, array_handle_ptr);
+zb.writeObjectRef(mem, offset, object_handle_ptr);
+```
+
+## Layout Constants
+
+These match `src/types.ts` exactly:
+
+| Constant | Value | Description |
+|---|---|---|
+| `VALUE_SLOT` | 16 | Size of a tagged value slot |
+| `STRING_HEADER` | 4 | String: `[byteLength: u32]` then UTF-8 bytes |
+| `ARRAY_HEADER` | 8 | Array: `[capacity: u32, length: u32]` then value slots |
+| `OBJECT_HEADER` | 8 | Object: `[capacity: u32, count: u32]` then entries |
+| `OBJECT_ENTRY` | 24 | Entry: `[keyPtr: u32, keyLen: u32, value: VALUE_SLOT]` |

@@ -0,0 +1,62 @@
+---
+title: zerobuf
+description: Zero-copy JS ↔ WASM data exchange via shared memory.
+template: splash
+hero:
+  tagline: Both sides read and write the same bytes. Zero copy, zero serialization.
+  actions:
+    - text: Get Started
+      link: /zerobuf/getting-started/
+      icon: right-arrow
+      variant: primary
+    - text: Memory Layout Spec
+      link: /zerobuf/spec/memory-layout/
+      variant: minimal
+---
+
+import { Card, CardGrid } from "@astrojs/starlight/components";
+
+## Why zerobuf
+
+Every WASM tool today copies data across the JS/WASM boundary. wasm-bindgen, AssemblyScript, Emscripten — all serialize in, copy, deserialize out. Two full copies per call.
+
+zerobuf eliminates this. JS and WASM share one `WebAssembly.Memory`. Both sides agree on a binary layout. Proxy objects on the JS side read/write directly to WASM linear memory.
+
+<CardGrid>
+  <Card title="Zero Copy" icon="rocket">
+    No serialization, no memcpy. JS Proxy objects read/write WASM memory directly.
+    `obj.x` reads from `wasm.memory`, not from a JS copy.
+  </Card>
+  <Card title="Lazy Materialization" icon="magnifier">
+    A 10MB object tree in WASM memory costs zero JS heap until you read a specific field.
+    Only the fields you touch are decoded.
+  </Card>
+  <Card title="Dynamic Objects" icon="pencil">
+    Objects grow, arrays grow, strings change length. Push, extend, add properties —
+    all in WASM memory with automatic realloc.
+  </Card>
+  <Card title="Multi-Language" icon="translate">
+    Binary layout spec with native implementations per language.
+    Zig, Rust, Go, Python — each reads/writes the same bytes.
+  </Card>
+</CardGrid>
+
+## Quick Example
+
+```typescript
+import { zerobuf } from "zerobuf";
+
+const buf = zerobuf(wasm.exports.memory);
+
+// Create object directly in WASM linear memory
+const point = buf.create({ x: 1.0, y: 2.0, label: "origin" });
+
+// Proxy reads/writes WASM memory — no copy
+point.x = 3.14;
+
+// Pass pointer to WASM function
+transform(point.__zerobuf_ptr);
+
+// WASM wrote result in-place — JS reads it back, still zero copy
+console.log(point.x);
+```

@@ -0,0 +1,129 @@
+---
+title: Memory Layout Spec
+description: The binary format that both JS and WASM agree on.
+---
+
+## Overview
+
+zerobuf defines a binary layout in `WebAssembly.Memory` that any language can read/write. This page is the spec — if you're implementing a binding for a new language, this is all you need.
+
+All values are **little-endian**. All offsets are byte offsets from the start of WASM linear memory.
+
+## Tagged Value Slot (16 bytes)
+
+Every value occupies a fixed 16-byte slot:
+
+```
+Offset  Size  Field
+0       1     tag (enum, see below)
+1-3     3     padding (unused)
+4       4     payload A (i32/bool/ptr)
+8       8     payload B (f64)
+```
+
+The tag determines which payload fields are used:
+
+| Tag | Value | Payload A (offset +4) | Payload B (offset +8) |
+|---|---|---|---|
+| `Null` | 0 | unused | unused |
+| `Bool` | 1 | 0 or 1 (u32) | unused |
+| `I32` | 2 | value (i32) | unused |
+| `F64` | 3 | unused | value (f64) |
+| `String` | 4 | string header ptr (u32) | unused |
+| `Array` | 5 | array handle ptr (u32) | unused |
+| `Object` | 6 | object handle ptr (u32) | unused |
+
+## String
+
+```
+Offset  Size       Field
+0       4          byteLength (u32) — UTF-8 byte count
+4       byteLength UTF-8 bytes (no null terminator)
+```
+
+Strings are immutable once written. To change a string property, allocate a new string and update the pointer in the value slot.
+
+## Array
+
+Arrays use **handle indirection** — a 4-byte cell holds the current data pointer. When the array grows beyond capacity, a new data block is allocated and the handle is updated. Proxies capture the handle address, so they survive realloc.
+
+### Handle (4 bytes)
+
+```
+Offset  Size  Field
+0       4     data pointer (u32) — points to array data
+```
+
+### Data Block
+
+```
+Offset               Size        Field
+0                    4           capacity (u32)
+4                    4           length (u32)
+8                    16 * cap    value slots (VALUE_SLOT each)
+```
+
+Element `i` is at data offset `8 + i * 16`.
+
+### Growth
+
+When `length == capacity`, allocate a new data block with `capacity * 2`, copy existing elements, write the new element, and update the handle to point to the new data. Old data is abandoned (arena is append-only).
+
+## Object
+
+Objects also use handle indirection, same as arrays.
+
+### Handle (4 bytes)
+
+```
+Offset  Size  Field
+0       4     data pointer (u32) — points to object data
+```
+
+### Data Block
+
+```
+Offset               Size         Field
+0                    4            capacity (u32)
+4                    4            count (u32) — number of properties
+8                    24 * cap     entries (OBJECT_ENTRY each)
+```
+
+### Entry (24 bytes)
+
+```
+Offset  Size  Field
+0       4     key pointer (u32) — points to UTF-8 key bytes in memory
+4       4     key byte length (u32)
+8       16    value (VALUE_SLOT — tagged value)
+```
+
+Property lookup is **linear scan** — compare key bytes for each entry. This is O(n) in property count, which is fine for typical object sizes (< 100 properties). Schema mode (planned) will provide O(1) fixed-offset access.
+
+### Growth
+
+Same as arrays: when `count == capacity`, allocate `capacity * 2`, copy entries, append new entry, update handle.
+
+## Arena Allocator
+
+All allocations use a bump allocator over `WebAssembly.Memory`:
+
+- **Alignment**: `offset = (offset + align - 1) & ~(align - 1)` before each alloc
+- **Growth**: doubling strategy — `memory.grow(max(needed, current * 2))`
+- **Max**: 65535 pages (4GB minus 64KB — avoids Chrome unsigned overflow at exactly 4GB)
+- **No free**: arena is append-only. Realloc allocates new and abandons old.
+
+### Shared Arena State (planned)
+
+For Zig/Rust WASM modules that need to allocate zerobuf objects, the arena offset will be stored at a fixed address in memory. Both JS and WASM read/write the same offset — no import call needed.
+
+## Alignment
+
+| Type | Alignment |
+|---|---|
+| Value slot | 4 bytes |
+| String header | 1 byte |
+| String bytes | 1 byte |
+| Array/object data | 4 bytes |
+| Handle | 4 bytes |
+| F64 payload | aligned to offset +8 within the 16-byte slot |
